figures.caption: Figure 3: Accuracies and average number of generated tokens during the reasoning process across math, scientific QA, and coding benchmarks. Round 1 represents isolated agents. Insight libraries learned improve agents’ reasoning capabilities across different disciplines while reducing the number of problem-solving tokens.

Figure 8: Comparison of FoT, isolated agents, and RAG in their ability to generate insights that can cover the core contributions of ICLR 2025 papers (guidance rate) under varying numbers of generated insights. Local agents use Gemini-2-Flash as the base LLM and are fed with ICLR 2024 papers as input. FoT generates higher-quality insights that better guide future research directions while requiring less input text, hence reducing token consumption.
Figure 1: Workflow of federation over (accumulated) gradients and federation over text. See pseudocode in Algorithms 1 and 2 in the appendix.

Table 5: Accuracies of math problem solving (LiveMathBench) under different settings. With the FoT-generated library, agents can achieve higher accuracies relative to solving the problems in an isolated manner. ‘local 1’ refers to using our curated prompts in Appendix D for local thinking, while ‘local 2’ refers to the method that directly adopts Didolkar et al. (2025) for local reasoning.
Table 1: Comparison of Federated Learning over Gradients and Federation over Text (FoT).

Figure 5: Comparison of reasoning efficiency and accuracy, averaged over eight math benchmarks in Table 5. We see that under two different choices of local reasoning approaches, FoT reduces reasoning loops and tokens generated when solving the tasks.

Figure 6: Accuracy and number of output tokens using different server-side algorithms on LiveMathBench. FoT achieves the highest accuracy with a moderate number of tokens, providing the best trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.

Figure 7: Percentage of papers guided by insights using different server-side algorithms. FoT can generate research ideas guiding most papers with a reasonable size of insight library. General context compression methods work but are not most suitable for the task.

Figure 2: Comparison of reasoning accuracies and efficiency (measured by average number of reasoning tokens and total loops), averaged over eight math benchmarks in Table 2. With the presence of an insight library, agents generate fewer tokens during the reasoning processes while achieving better final performance. Additionally, FoT results in fewer loops, i.e., reducing the occurrence of repeated content in reasoning.
